repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem: There is no way for a `vim.ui.input` caller to indicate for which scope the input is. As in "This input is for something at cursor scope". This information can be useful for `vim.ui.input` implementation to tweak its behavior and presentation:
  - Show different floating window depending on the scope. For example:
    - Near cursor for "cursor" scope.
    - At line start for "line" scope.
    - In window corner for "buffer" and "window" scope.
    - In whole editor corner for "tabpage" and "editor" scope.
  - Navigate through history only for inputs with the same scope.

Solution: Document new `opts.scope` for `vim.ui.input`. Use it in the codebase.

---

Related to #37511.
Similar in spirit to #37360.

comment_data.body: It might be more appropriate to call it `relative` (or `rel`) instead of `scope`, as this would match the parameter of `nvim_open_win`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
I don't think this is a good idea:
1) This is intentionally has nothing to do with floating windows. It *can*, but it shouldn't.
2) Values of `relative` in `nvim_open_win()` are for UI components, which does not have to be related.
3) This is closer by its goal to `opts.scope` in `:h vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`, which uses name `scope`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
> 1\. This is intentionally has nothing to do with floating windows. It _can_, but it shouldn't.

As you mentioned in the PR description:
> Show different floating window depending on the scope.

Doesn't this clarify the association with floating windows?

> 2\. Values of `relative` in `nvim_open_win()` are for UI components, which does not have to be related.

As I understand it, `vim.ui.input` is simply a UI component provided as an interface so that plugins can reimplement it by overriding. 

> 3\. This is closer by its goal to `opts.scope` in `:h vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`, which uses name `scope`.

This is fundamentally different from the `scope` in `vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`. In that case, `scope` determines the range of diagnostics to be displayed in a newly opened floating window—for example, `cursor` includes diagnostics under the current cursor in the floating window, while `buffer` includes diagnostics from the entire buffer. That seems even further removed from the description here.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
> Doesn't this clarify the association with floating windows?

It *can* be related to a floating window position, but it *doesn't have to*. It is possible to implement it via `echo`, inline extmark, text in virtual line, statusline, etc.

> As I understand it, `vim.ui.input` is simply a UI component provided as an interface so that plugins can reimplement it by overriding.

It is. Yet `opts.scope` can be used not only for UI decisions. Some example:
- Incorporate it when computing history and/or completion candidates.
- If there is no `opts.default`, auto populate input area with the latest history input from the same scope.

In both cases the sentence "suggest history entry that had the same input scope" sounds clearer than "suggest history entry that had the same relative input".

> This is fundamentally different from the `scope` in `vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`. In that case, `scope` determines the range of diagnostics to be displayed in a newly opened floating window ...

It is not fundamentally different. As explained above, `opts.scope` can also be reasonably used for something else than UI decisions. And in a way similar to how it is used in `vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body: probably need a (tiny) concrete example. maybe something like, `For example, the input may highlight the word near the cursor if scope=cursor`. But I'm wondering if that's actually the role of an input...

+                        (`"cursor"|"line"|"buffer"|"window"|"tabpage"|"editor"`)

+                        cursor/line/etc scope". Can be used by `vim.ui.input`
+                        implementations to tweak behavior and presentation.

comment_data.body:
WDYT about defining this as a generic `@alias` ? the right place seems to be in `runtime/lua/vim/_meta/builtin.lua` .

Similar to `vim.pos` and `vim.uri`, this seems like it might be a common concept.

As a followup, hopefully it makes sense to also use that type in `vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -98,14 +98,19 @@ end
 ---Function that will be used for highlighting
 ---user inputs.
 ---@field highlight? function
+---
+---Input scope, as in "This input is for something at cursor/line/etc scope".
+---Can be used by `vim.ui.input` implementations to tweak behavior and presentation.
+---@field scope? "cursor"|"line"|"buffer"|"window"|"tabpage"|"editor"

comment_data.body:
> In #25509 I proposed something like `vim.pos.rel()` but it sounds like it won't fit here.

I indeed don't think it is. Using "relative" and "relative kind" to me heavily implies a that it is for something UI related. Probably because: 1) it is used in `nvim_open_win`; and 2) it has values named after UI elements (`laststatus`, `tabline`, `mouse`).

The proposed "scope" notion/enum is more about hierarchical structure of "zones of responsibility" that are present in Nvim. In fact, I had a really hard time to not use the word "scope" to describe what I mean, because it fits perfectly here :)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
Sure, sounds reasonable. 

> As a followup, hopefully it makes sense to also use that type in `vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`

It makes sense at least from the implementation point of view:
- Scope `'window'` would get diagnostics from the current window. I.e. the same as `'buffer'` scope.
- Scope `'tabpage'` would get diagnostics from all the windows in the current tabpage.
- Scope `'editor'` would get diagnostics from all (listed?) buffers.

For this to be more useful in `vim.diagnostic.Opts.Float`, there might also be something like `'project'` scope. Which can also fit in the `vim.ui.input` use case.

What I would really like to avoid is having to allow single letter scopes. It is kind of okay, but adds unnecessary constraints on possible new future scopes.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -98,14 +98,19 @@ end
 ---Function that will be used for highlighting
 ---user inputs.
 ---@field highlight? function
+---
+---Input scope, as in "This input is for something at cursor/line/etc scope".
+---Can be used by `vim.ui.input` implementations to tweak behavior and presentation.
+---@field scope? "cursor"|"line"|"buffer"|"window"|"tabpage"|"editor"

comment_data.body: yeah we don't need the single letter stuff. we can soft-deprecate that in diagnostic, and it doesn't need to be part of the documented interface.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -98,14 +98,19 @@ end

 ---user inputs.
 ---@field highlight? function
+---
+---Input scope, as in "This input is for something at cursor/line/etc scope".
+---Can be used by `vim.ui.input` implementations to tweak behavior and presentation.
+---@field scope? "cursor"|"line"|"buffer"|"window"|"tabpage"|"editor"

comment_data.body:
type names don't matter too much (no contract), but I don't think we use `vim.builtin` anywhere, and don't want to start that trend

```suggestion
--- @alias vim.Scope
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -255,3 +255,12 @@ function vim.ui_attach(ns, opts, callback) end
 --- given namespace {ns}.
 --- @param ns integer Namespace ID
 function vim.ui_detach(ns) end
+
+--- @alias vim.builtin.Scope

comment_data.body: Force pushed the name change. Still not sure how to make available scope values be documented at least somewhere, ideally in `vim.ui.input` itself.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -255,3 +255,12 @@ function vim.ui_attach(ns, opts, callback) end
 --- given namespace {ns}.
 --- @param ns integer Namespace ID
 function vim.ui_detach(ns) end
+
+--- @alias vim.builtin.Scope

comment_data.body:
I am still confused by your description. Do you think that `vim.ui.input` is solely a UI component? It seems like you are saying yes at times and no at others.

> It is not fundamentally different. 

The fundamentally different thing I mentioned above is the level at which the service is provided. The key point is that `vim.diagnostic.open_float()` reaches the user directly, and the user passes the `scope` themselves. In contrast, `vim.ui.input` does not; it serves plugins, and nvim users are unlikely to call it directly. To be more specific:
- For `vim.diagnostic.open_float()`
  ```
  user -> vim.diagnostic.open_float({ scope = "buffer" }) -> Complete
  ```
- For `vim.ui.input()`
  ```
  user -> vim.lsp.buf.rename() -> vim.ui.input() -> callback in vim.lsp.buf.rename() -> Complete
  ```
As you can see, the plugin first calls `vim.ui.input`, and its result must still be processed by the plugin's callback before interacting with the user. Given this, why would a plugin use `vim.ui.input` instead of implementing its own input box? Because its exact purpose is to unify input boxes in nvim, allowing users to define the appearances of all input boxes in one place.

I still believe this is the sole purpose of `vim.ui.input`, so adding logic beyond UI decisions to it is not necessary. To give another example, if the `vim.lsp.buf.rename` mentioned above were to set an `opts.scope`, the call chain would look like this:
```
user -> vim.lsp.buf.rename() -> vim.ui.input({ scope = "buffer" }) -> callback in vim.lsp.buf.rename()
```
As you can see, even if `rename` provides a parameter like `scope = "buffer"` to `vim.ui.input`, the functionality beyond the UI is still controlled by the callback it passes to `vim.ui.input`. In that case, why should we pass this parameter to `vim.ui.input` and let it take a meaningless detour? If supports, users can pass `scope = "buffer" to `vim.lsp.buf.rename()`. After all, the logic beyond the UI decisions is still processed by the plugin itself; `vim.ui.input` does not need to know anything beyond UI.

> `opts.scope` can also be reasonably used for something else than UI decisions.

So, as I explained above, I think adding something else UI decisions to `vim.ui.input` is not reasonable. 

I can only see one use case outside of UI decisions that is reasonable: the support for search history you mentioned in the PR description. However, if we truly need to provide such support, I think it would be better to add a `source` to record the caller, which would be more beneficial for users searching through history. Could you provide another specific use case?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
> I am still confused by your description. Do you think that `vim.ui.input` is solely a UI component? It seems like you are saying yes at times and no at others.

It is an adjustable way to interactively ask user for the input. Adjusting comes by user overriding (manually or via plugin) `vim.ui.input` in a way that follows the API spec.

> I still believe this is the sole purpose of `vim.ui.input`, so adding logic beyond UI decisions to it is not necessary.

Nothing is *necessary* per se, but something can still be useful. 

> As you can see, even if `rename` provides a parameter like `scope = "buffer"` to `vim.ui.input`, the functionality beyond the UI is still controlled by the callback it passes to `vim.ui.input`.

Acting on user input is a functionality done *after* user has finished an input. It is still reasonable to want to adjust input functionality *during* making such input.

The relatively high level `scope` allows doing that while not adding too much to the API spec. Adjusting floating window position is its possible UI application. Adjusting features *during* input is its other possible application:
- Providing more relevant history.
- Providing more relevant completion candidates if there was no explicit `opts.completion`. Like keywords from current buffer if `scope <= 'window'`, from current tabpage for `'tabpage'`, all buffers for `'editor'`, whole project for `'project'`.

> However, if we truly need to provide such support, I think it would be better to add a `source` to record the caller, which would be more beneficial for users searching through history.

Adding `src` would be indeed useful. But that is tangential to what is being discussed here. It is a perfectly reasonable for a `vim.ui.input` implementation to try to suggest something if there was no input for this particular source. Having a `scope` field could make such history suggestions more useful, if the implementation wants to add it.

---

I think this discussion goes a bit out of hand, by the way. If the only problem you have is the `scope` name, then we can agree to disagree. I think it is a very fitting name for the purpose and it is general enough to also be useful outside of `vim.ui.input`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
> If the only problem you have is the `scope` name, then we can agree to disagree. I think it is a very fitting name for the purpose and it is general enough to also be useful outside of `vim.ui.input`.

The problem I am exploring is whether we should allow this newly added field—regardless of whether it is called `scope` or `relative`—to have functionality beyond describing UI behavior; specifically, should it truly provide this kind of general utility? I am inclined to limit it strictly to controlling UI behavior, it's just if this is the case we finally choose, `relative` would be more appropriate than `scope`.

This might be a matter of personal preference or programming philosophy. For me personally, if I want to provide a general-purpose API, I prefer to find a concept that is inherently general enough, rather than trying to make a specific concept general. To me, such an API brings ambiguity rather than true generality.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
> The problem I am exploring is whether we should allow this newly added field—regardless of whether it is called `scope` or `relative`—to have functionality beyond describing UI behavior; specifically, should it truly provide this kind of general utility?

I don't see any reason to restrict what `vim.ui.input()` implementations are allowed to do with information passed by the user. It is not that currently there is a requirement that `vim.ui.input` implementations *must* adjust certain behavior based on the `scope` value. They can completely ignore it if they'd like to.

Besides, if `vim.ui.input` should *only* be allowed a UI related configuration, should both `opts.completion` and `opts.default` be removed? I would say "Of course no", as they enabled very useful non-UI related functionality.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body:
> Besides, if `vim.ui.input` should _only_ be allowed a UI related configuration, should both `opts.completion` and `opts.default` be removed? I would say "Of course no", as they enabled very useful non-UI related functionality.

My take on this is that `completion` and `default` will affect the implementation of `vim.ui.input`. Plugins implementing `vim.ui.input` need to know the values of `default` and `completion` to display the content; whereas for `scope` here, the plugin only needs to know where the input box should be positioned, which is why my initial suggestion was to narrow its meaning to `relative`.

However, my discussion here is focused on clarifying my doubts regarding the meaning of `scope` here, rather than blocking this PR. I think we have both made our respective points clear, so I think we can agree to disagree, as you said.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5129,6 +5130,11 @@ vim.ui.input({opts}, {on_confirm})                            *vim.ui.input()*
                       • {highlight}? (`function`) Function that will be used
                         for highlighting user inputs.
                       • {prompt}? (`string`) Text of the prompt
+                      • {scope}?

comment_data.body: Nit: I would keep the `prompt` inline.

comment_data.body:
Technically: whatever the implementation decides it to be. The same as as for all other fields, as neither has explicit default values defined for them.

Practically: I think `'editor'` is a good default value. This can be added here, of course. Or not.

comment_data.body:
But it will then be too wide for help buffer.

Does this look better?

```lua
local opts = { prompt = 'Enter value for shiftwidth: ', scope = 'buffer' }
vim.ui.input(opts, function(input)
    vim.o.shiftwidth = tonumber(input)
end)
```

+        local prompt = 'Enter value for shiftwidth: '

comment_regular_data.body: I've moved the scope enum to be an `@alias` (as per this comment), but I am not sure how to properly generate documentation so that the allowed values of the `scope` is shown somewhere. Preferably inline in `vim.ui.input`. @justinmk, what is the assumed approach here?

> Force pushed the name change. Still not sure how to make available scope values be documented at least somewhere, ideally in `vim.ui.input` itself.

If `@inlinedoc` doesn't work then we may need to figure out how to get it to work. Backlog issue or followup pr.

> If `@inlinedoc` doesn't work then we may need to figure out how to get it to work. Backlog issue or followup pr.

There is already an `@inlinedoc` for `vim.ui.input.Opts`. Adding `@inlinedoc` to `vim.Scope` doesn't seem to do anything.
I pushed an explicit documentation of allowed values without `--- @alias`. Could not find a way to generate a documentation for that, so opened a #39752.

title: feat(ui): add and use `opts.scope` in `vim.ui.input`
